taxonomy.name: Under-Range Constraint (URC)
taxonomy.description: A signal wire w in the circuit has its range constraint underspecified. The prover can assign w a value outside its intended domain (e.g., w > 2^k for a k-bit signal) without causing a constraint failure. In ZKML circuits, this most commonly affects quantized activation values (e.g., ReLU output clamped to [0, 255] in int8 quantization).
taxonomy.formal_condition: ∃ wire w with intended domain D_w = [0, 2^k - 1] where the circuit only asserts: w * (w - 1) = 0 (binary check) but NOT: w ∈ [0, 2^k - 1] via range-check gates. Then prover can set w = 2^k + δ for any δ, satisfying all constraints.
taxonomy.soundness_violation: Validator submits y* = f_fake(x) where intermediate activations use overflow values. The verifier's constraints pass (no range check), but the computation is not a valid execution of f(x).
taxonomy.mitigation:
CONSTRAINT TEMPLATE (Range Check):
  For each wire w with domain [0, B]:
    (1) Decompose w into k bits: w = Σ bᵢ · 2ⁱ
    (2) Assert each bit: bᵢ · (bᵢ - 1) = 0
    (3) Assert upper bound: B - w ≥ 0 (via auxiliary range-check circuit)
  EZKL implementation: use lookup tables for range enforcement.

taxonomy.name: Non-Deterministic Witness Assignment (NDWA)
taxonomy.description: The circuit has multiple valid witness assignments for the same input x, all satisfying all constraints, but producing different outputs y. This occurs when the circuit models a non-injective function without uniqueness constraints — common in argmax layers and attention mechanisms.
taxonomy.formal_condition:
∃ input x, witnesses w₁ ≠ w₂ such that:
  C(x, w₁) is satisfiable with output y₁
  C(x, w₂) is satisfiable with output y₂
  y₁ ≠ y₂
Then the circuit is unsound: multiple valid outputs exist for same input.
taxonomy.soundness_violation: Malicious validator picks the witness assignment w₂ that maximizes their Trust Score (highest accuracy claim) while using a fake computation.
taxonomy.mitigation:
CONSTRAINT TEMPLATE (Uniqueness Enforcement):
  For argmax output i* = argmax(logits):
    (1) Assert: logits[i*] ≥ logits[j] + ε for all j ≠ i*
    (2) Assert indicator: δᵢ ∈ {0,1}, Σδᵢ = 1 (one-hot check)
    (3) Assert: i* = Σ i · δᵢ (index consistency)
  This eliminates non-determinism by enforcing strict ordering.

taxonomy.name: Missing Intermediate Constraint (MIC)
taxonomy.description: Intermediate computation results are computed correctly by an honest prover but their correctness is never asserted in the constraint system. A malicious prover can set intermediate wires to arbitrary values while keeping inputs and outputs consistent — effectively 'skipping' layers of the network.
taxonomy.formal_condition:
Let f = fₙ ∘ fₙ₋₁ ∘ ... ∘ f₁ be a composed neural network.
If the circuit only asserts: C_out(x, y) without asserting C_layer_k for some intermediate layer k, then the prover can set:
  h_k = arbitrary value (not f_k(h_{k-1}))
  h_{k+1} = f_{k+1}(h_k) ... h_n = y*
The circuit accepts y* even though layer k was bypassed.
taxonomy.soundness_violation: Validator submits y* as if running model f, but actually runs a simplified model that skips expensive layers (e.g., transformer blocks), achieving low computational cost while claiming full model credit.
taxonomy.mitigation:
CONSTRAINT TEMPLATE (Layer Consistency):
  For each layer k with input hₖ and output hₖ₊₁:
    Assert: hₖ₊₁ = fₖ(hₖ) via a dedicated sub-circuit
  Implementation: Use recursive SNARK composition (e.g., Nova, Halo2)
  or EZKL's full trace enforcement mode (--full-witness flag).

taxonomy.name: Floating-Point Approximation Exploit (FPAE)
taxonomy.description: ZK circuits operate over finite fields (typically Fp for large prime p). Neural networks use floating-point arithmetic. The quantization/dequantization step introduces approximation gaps that a malicious prover can exploit: by choosing quantization parameters (scale, zero_point) adversarially, they can shift model outputs while remaining within the circuit's approximation tolerance bounds.
taxonomy.formal_condition:
Let Q: ℝ → ℤ be quantization with scale s and zero_point z.
The circuit checks: |Q_circuit(x) - Q_honest(x)| ≤ ε (tolerance).
If the adversary controls s and z, they can choose:
  s* = s + δ such that Q*(x) ≠ Q(x) but |Q*(x) - Q(x)| ≤ ε
  This shifts logits systematically while passing the tolerance check.
taxonomy.soundness_violation: Validator manipulates quantization parameters to boost claimed accuracy metric while running a degraded model. The systematic shift can change classification outcomes for targeted input classes.
taxonomy.mitigation:
CONSTRAINT TEMPLATE (Fixed Quantization Parameters):
  (1) Commit to quantization parameters: H(s, z) published on-chain
  (2) Assert in circuit: s = s_committed, z = z_committed
  (3) Use lookup tables for quantized ops (eliminates approximation gap)
  (4) Bound tolerance tightly: ε < 0.5 (rounding only, no systematic shift)
cve_analogues: Numerical precision exploit in oracle price manipulation

taxonomy.name: Commitment-Output Mismatch (COM)
taxonomy.description: The validator commits to a model hash H(θ) on-chain but the ZKML circuit does not verify that the weights used in the proof match H(θ). A malicious validator can thus generate a proof using a different (weaker) model θ* while claiming credit for the committed model θ.
taxonomy.formal_condition:
Let H: Θ → {0,1}^256 be a collision-resistant hash of model weights.
The validator publishes c = H(θ) on-chain.
If the circuit does NOT assert: weights_in_proof = θ where H(θ) = c,
then the prover can use weights θ* ≠ θ to generate a valid proof.
taxonomy.soundness_violation: Validator runs a tiny fast model θ* (e.g., 1-layer MLP) instead of the committed large model θ (e.g., ResNet-50), generates a valid ZK proof for θ*, and submits it as proof of running θ. The verifier cannot distinguish.
taxonomy.mitigation:
CONSTRAINT TEMPLATE (Weight Commitment Verification):
  (1) Merkle-commit all weight tensors: root = MerkleRoot({Wₖ})
  (2) Publish root on-chain at registration
  (3) In each proof: assert Merkle path for each weight tensor used
  (4) Assert: MerkleRoot(weights_in_proof) = committed_root
  EZKL supports this via --witness-includes-weights mode.

soundness_conditions.V-I: ∀ wire w ∈ activation layer: assert w ∈ [0, 2^k - 1] via range-check subcircuit
soundness_conditions.V-II: ∀ argmax output i*: assert uniqueness via strict ordering constraints
soundness_conditions.V-III: ∀ layer k: assert h_{k+1} = f_k(h_k) via dedicated per-layer subcircuit
soundness_conditions.V-IV: Commit to (s, z) on-chain; assert (s, z) = (s_committed, z_committed) in proof
soundness_conditions.V-V: Assert MerkleRoot(weights_in_proof) = committed_root for every proof

poc_results.title: Under-Range Constraint Exploit on int8 ReLU

rq2_answer: Five classes of underconstrained ZKML circuit vulnerabilities identified in the PoUI validator context. Necessary and sufficient conditions derived for each class. Class V-I and V-V exploit PoCs constructed. Constraint templates provided for all five classes.
